evals.name: lru-cache-with-ttl
evals.prompt: Design and implement an LRU cache that also supports per-key TTL (time-to-live) expiration. The cache should support get(key), put(key, value, ttl_seconds), and delete(key) operations, all in O(1) average time. Expired keys should be lazily evicted on access and periodically cleaned up. The cache has a fixed maximum capacity. When the cache is full and a new key is inserted, the least recently used non-expired key should be evicted. Discuss the data structure choices, trade-offs between eager vs lazy expiration, memory overhead, and thread-safety considerations.
evals.reference_answer: The optimal approach combines a hash map for O(1) key lookup with a doubly linked list for O(1) LRU ordering, plus a sorted structure (min-heap or sorted set keyed by expiration timestamp) for efficient TTL expiration. For the core LRU: HashMap<Key, Node> where Node contains key, value, expiry_timestamp, and prev/next pointers in the doubly linked list. get(key): look up in hashmap, check if expired (current_time > expiry), if expired remove and return miss, otherwise move to head of list and return value. put(key, value, ttl): if key exists update in-place, otherwise create node, add to hashmap and head of list, add to expiry heap; if over capacity, evict from tail of list (skipping expired). delete(key): remove from hashmap, unlink from list, mark as deleted in heap (lazy deletion). For TTL cleanup: lazy approach checks expiry on every get/put; periodic cleanup thread walks the min-heap popping expired entries. Trade-offs: eager expiration (background thread) uses more CPU but keeps memory tighter; lazy expiration is simpler but can accumulate expired entries. Memory overhead: ~3 pointers per entry (prev, next, heap position) plus the hashmap entry. Thread safety: reader-writer lock on the cache, or lock-free with CAS on the list head for high-throughput scenarios. Alternative approaches: OrderedDict in Python gives LRU but no TTL without a separate heap; Redis-style approach with a probabilistic expiry sampler. Edge cases: TTL=0 (immediate expiry), put with same key but different TTL, capacity=1, all keys expired simultaneously, clock skew in distributed settings.

evals.name: median-in-streaming-data
evals.prompt: Design a data structure that supports the following operations on a stream of integers: addNum(int num) which adds a number to the stream, and findMedian() which returns the median of all numbers added so far. Both operations should be as efficient as possible. The stream can contain up to 10^8 numbers, with values ranging from -10^5 to 10^5. Discuss multiple approaches with their trade-offs, analyze time and space complexity rigorously, and address what changes if the data distribution is heavily skewed or if approximate answers are acceptable.
evals.reference_answer: The classic optimal approach uses two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half, maintaining the invariant that sizes differ by at most 1. addNum: compare with max-heap top, push to appropriate heap, rebalance if sizes differ by more than 1. findMedian: if equal sizes return average of both tops, otherwise return top of larger heap. Time: O(log n) add, O(1) find. Space: O(n). Alternative 1: Balanced BST (AVL/Red-Black) with order statistics — O(log n) for both operations, can also support delete and range queries, but more complex to implement. Alternative 2: Since values are bounded [-10^5, 10^5], use a counting sort / frequency array of size 200,001. addNum is O(1), findMedian walks the array to find the middle element in O(range) = O(200,001) = O(1) amortized with a maintained running count and pointer. This is actually the fastest for this specific constraint. Alternative 3: For approximate median, use P-squared algorithm or t-digest, giving O(1) add and O(1) find with bounded error. Trade-offs: two-heap is the general-purpose gold standard; frequency array exploits the bounded range for better constants; BST adds flexibility; approximation algorithms trade accuracy for speed. For skewed distributions: the two-heap approach is unaffected by skew; the frequency array pointer will cluster in one region making scans faster; t-digest handles skew well by allocating more precision near the median. Edge cases: single element, two elements, all same value, alternating max/min values, overflow in sum for average computation.

evals.name: concurrent-skip-list-vs-btree
evals.prompt: You are building an in-memory ordered key-value store that must support concurrent reads and writes from multiple threads. The operations needed are: insert(key, value), delete(key), get(key), and range_scan(start_key, end_key) which returns all key-value pairs in the range. Expected dataset size is 10M-100M entries. Compare and recommend between a concurrent skip list and a concurrent B+ tree for this use case. Analyze lock granularity, cache performance, memory overhead, and scalability under high contention. Provide pseudocode for the recommended approach's insert operation with its concurrency control.
evals.reference_answer: For concurrent in-memory ordered KV stores at 10M-100M scale, the skip list is generally preferred over B+ tree. Here's why: Lock granularity: Skip lists allow fine-grained locking — each node can be locked independently during insert/delete, and readers never need locks (with atomic pointer reads). B+ trees require locking at page/node granularity, and structural modifications (splits/merges) can lock entire subtrees. Skip lists achieve higher concurrency. Cache performance: B+ trees win here — their node layout is cache-line friendly (sequential keys in each node, high fanout reduces tree height). Skip lists have poor spatial locality (pointer chasing across memory). For pure read performance, B+ trees are 2-5x faster. Memory overhead: Skip list nodes average 2 pointers per level with expected 2 levels per node = ~4 pointers per entry. B+ tree nodes have fill factor overhead but amortize pointer costs across many keys. B+ trees typically use less memory. Scalability under contention: Skip lists scale better under write-heavy workloads due to structural simplicity — no splits/merges propagating up the tree. B+ trees suffer from contention at internal nodes during splits. For read-heavy workloads, B+ trees can use optimistic locking (version counters) effectively. Recommendation: Skip list for write-heavy or mixed workloads; B+ tree with optimistic concurrency for read-heavy workloads with range scans. For the 10M-100M scale with concurrent reads/writes, a lock-free skip list (like Java's ConcurrentSkipListMap) is the pragmatic choice. Pseudocode for concurrent skip list insert: (1) Find predecessors and successors at each level using lock-free traversal, (2) Lock predecessors bottom-up, (3) Validate that predecessors still point to successors (no concurrent modification), (4) If validation fails, retry from step 1, (5) Insert new node by linking at each level bottom-up, (6) Unlock. Edge cases: duplicate keys (update semantics), empty ranges, concurrent insert and delete of same key, iterator invalidation during range scan, level generation randomness (geometric distribution with p=0.5 or 0.25).

evals.name: string-matching-multiple-patterns
evals.prompt: You need to build a content moderation system that checks incoming text messages (average 500 characters, up to 10K) against a dictionary of 50,000 banned phrases (ranging from single words to 5-word phrases). The system must process 10,000 messages per second with sub-millisecond latency per message. Design the data structure and algorithm for this. Compare at least three approaches, analyze their preprocessing time, query time, and memory usage. Address how to handle case-insensitive matching, Unicode normalization, and dynamic updates to the banned phrases dictionary.
evals.reference_answer: This is a classic multi-pattern string matching problem. Three main approaches: (1) Aho-Corasick automaton: Build a trie of all 50K patterns, add failure links (like KMP for multiple patterns), scan text once in O(n + m + z) where n=text length, m=total pattern length, z=matches. Preprocessing: O(m * alphabet_size) time and space. Query: O(n + z) per message — single pass, optimal. Memory: ~2-5MB for 50K patterns depending on alphabet. This is the gold standard for this problem. (2) Rabin-Karp with multiple hash sets: Compute rolling hashes for each possible substring length present in the dictionary. For each length L present in patterns, slide a window of size L across the text, compute hash, check against HashSet of pattern hashes. Query: O(n * k) where k is the number of distinct pattern lengths. Good when k is small (here phrases are 1-5 words so ~5-20 distinct lengths). Preprocessing: O(m). Memory: O(m) for hash sets. Less optimal than Aho-Corasick but simpler to implement. (3) Bloom filter pre-screen + exact match: For each distinct length L, maintain a Bloom filter of all L-length patterns. Slide window, check Bloom filter first (fast reject), on hit verify with exact HashSet lookup. Reduces average-case constant factor but worst-case same as Rabin-Karp. Good for very large dictionaries where most substrings are non-matches. Recommendation: Aho-Corasick. At 10K msgs/sec with 500-char average, that's 5M chars/sec — Aho-Corasick processes this trivially in single-threaded mode. Preprocessing the 50K patterns takes ~100ms once. For case-insensitive: lowercase both patterns and text during preprocessing/scanning. For Unicode normalization: apply NFC normalization to both patterns and text before building/querying the automaton. For dynamic updates: rebuild the automaton on dictionary change (50K patterns rebuilds in ~100ms, acceptable for infrequent updates), or maintain a small delta trie for recent additions and merge periodically. Edge cases: overlapping patterns, patterns that are prefixes of others, empty pattern, pattern longer than text, UTF-8 multi-byte characters affecting sliding window, homoglyph attacks (Cyrillic 'a' vs Latin 'a').
